content.full_text:
Ravi Mehta
Chairman

Fair Political Practices Commission

July 25, 1997

Richard Spencer
1104 Dennis Court
Rodeo, California 94572

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-325

Dear Mr. Spencer:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be
construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. Furthermore,
this letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does not act
as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May you, as a member of the Board of Directors of the Rodeo Sanitary District request
the district to vacate a portion of an easement it owns on a portion of a vacant lot you own?

CONCLUSION

You may not take any action in your official capacity to decide or influence any decision
relating to the easement, but you may appear before the Board of Directors of the Rodeo Sanitary
District to voice your position as an individual concerned citizen. You may not, however,
purport to represent any interests other than your personal interests.

FACTS

You are a member of the Board of Directors of the Rodeo Sanitary District. You also
own a vacant parcel within the boundaries of the district described as Parcel B of minor
subdivision 94005 located on Spruce Ct. in Rodeo. The Rodeo Sanitary District has a 33.18"
sewer easement on Parcel B. For development purposes you, as a private citizen, requested that

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-325
Page 2

the sanitary district vacate 25" of the existing easement. You were directed by the board of
directors to contact this Commission to find out how your request must be handled to avoid any
conflict of interest.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act was adopted by California voters through the initiative process
in 1974. Included within the Act are conflict-of-interest provisions intended to ensure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from any bias attributable to personal financial interests, or to the financial interests of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) To further this purpose, Section 87100 provides:

"No public official, at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest."

A "public official" is defined by the Act to include every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.) A public official "makes" or
"participates in making a governmental decision" when he or she votes on, approves, or
otherwise makes use of his or her official position to influence the outcome of a governmental
decision. (Regulations 18700(b) and (c); 18700.1.)

An official has a "financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on, among other
interests, any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$1000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

You are a public official by virtue of your membership on the Board of Directors of the
Rodeo Sanitary District. Your interest in the vacant lot is presumably worth $1000 or more.
You may not participate in a decision regarding the easement if the decision will foreseeably and
materially affect you or your real property interest in a manner that is distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally. (Harper Advice Letter, No. A-96-298.)

An effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. Certainty is not required, but the effect must be more than a mere possibility.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; Harper Advice Letter, No. 96-298.)
Presumably, if the easement is vacated, it will increase the development opportunities on the
land, and in so doing, increase its value.

File No. A-97-325
Page 3

Assuming the decision will have some financial effect on the fair market value of your
property, the effect must still be material to require disqualification. The Commission has
adopted a series of regulations which provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable
financial effects of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702; Larsen Advice Letter, No. I-91-
460.)

Under Regulation 18702.1, the effect of a decision is material when an official's
economic interest is directly involved in a decision. In this situation, the effect of the easement is
deemed material since the decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license,
permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of such property.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(B), copy enclosed; Ennis Advice Letter, No. A-93-341.)

Thus a decision regarding the sewer easement on your vacant lot would be deemed to
have a direct material effect on your property. Although you may not participate in this matter in
any official capacity, we see no barrier to appearances before the sanitary district as a member of
the general public, i.e., you may appear and speak in your capacity as an individual citizen,
representing yourself on matters related solely to your personal interests. The right of public
officials to speak as individual citizens on matters of purely personal interest is protected by
Regulations 18700(d)(2) and 18700.1(b)(1). You must be careful, however, to avoid giving the
impression that you are speaking in the interest of any person or group other than yourself and
your immediate family, or that you are acting in any official capacity. (Regulation 18701.1(b);
Harper Advice Letter, supra.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Meena Patel
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division

SGC:MP:jlw
Enclosure

5660.
